q: Which of these materials is naturally magnetic?
sol: Magnetite (also known as lodestone) is a natural magnetic mineral found in the Earth.

q: A magnet will always point in which direction when suspended freely?
sol: A freely suspended magnet always aligns itself along the Earth's North-South magnetic direction.

q: The property of a magnet to attract iron pieces is called:
sol: Magnetism is the force exerted by magnets when they attract or repel certain materials.

q: Which part of a bar magnet has the strongest magnetic pull?
sol: Magnetic strength is maximum at the two ends, known as the poles of the magnet.

q: What happens when the North pole of a magnet is brought near the North pole of another magnet?
sol: Like poles (N-N or S-S) always repel each other.

q: A compass needle is a:
sol: A compass consists of a tiny magnetized needle that can rotate freely.

q: Which of these is a non-magnetic material?
sol: Aluminum is not attracted by magnets, unlike iron, nickel, and cobalt.

q: To store bar magnets safely, they should be kept in pairs with:
sol: They should be kept in pairs with opposite poles on the same side, separated by wood or keepers.

q: If a magnet is broken into two pieces, each piece will have:
sol: Magnets always exist as dipoles; even a broken piece will have its own N and S poles.

q: The discovery of magnets is often associated with a shepherd named:
sol: Legend says a shepherd named Magnes discovered magnetite in ancient Greece.

q: Which of these can be used to make a temporary magnet?
sol: Soft iron is easily magnetized but loses its magnetism quickly, making it a temporary magnet.

q: What destroys the magnetic properties of a magnet?
sol: High heat, physical impact, and rough handling disalign the internal magnetic domains.

q: In ancient times, sailors used ________ to find directions.
sol: Lodestones (natural magnets) were used as primitive compasses by sailors.

q: A permanent magnet is usually made of:
sol: Steel and alloys like Alnico are used for permanent magnets because they retain magnetism for a long time.

q: Which pole of a compass needle usually points towards the Earth's North?
sol: The 'North-seeking' pole of the needle points to the geographical North.

q: Magnetic field lines emerge from the ________ and enter the ________.
sol: Field lines are conventionally defined to flow from North to South outside the magnet.

q: Magnets are NOT used in:
sol: Standard glass windows do not require or use magnetic properties.

q: A material that is weakly repelled by a magnet is called:
sol: Diamagnetic materials (like water or gold) are very weakly repelled by magnetic fields.

q: Which of the following is an artificial magnet?
sol: Bar magnets are manufactured by humans using magnetic materials.

q: Like poles ________ each other.
sol: Basic law of magnetism: Like poles repel, unlike poles attract.

q: The space around a magnet where its influence is felt is the:
sol: The magnetic field is the region where magnetic forces can be detected.

q: Which shape of magnet is commonly used in laboratories?
sol: The bar magnet is the most standard shape used for school experiments.

q: What happens if you rub an iron nail with a magnet in one direction several times?
sol: This is the single-touch method of making an artificial magnet.

q: Which of these is a magnetic metal?
sol: Nickel is one of the few metals that shows strong magnetic properties at room temperature.

q: A magnet attracts:
sol: Magnets attract ferromagnetic materials, primarily iron, nickel, and cobalt.

q: The Earth behaves like a giant magnet with its North magnetic pole near the:
sol: The Earth's magnetic North pole is actually located near the geographical South Pole (and vice-versa).

q: Can we isolate a single magnetic pole (monopole)?
sol: No, poles always come in pairs (Dipoles). You cannot have a North pole without a South pole.

q: Which device uses a magnet to show direction?
sol: The magnetic compass has been used for navigation for centuries.

q: The magnetic power of a magnet is concentrated at:
sol: The force is strongest near the poles.

q: When a magnet is heated to a very high temperature, it:
sol: Heat energy disrupts the alignment of magnetic domains.

q: Magnetic keepers are used to:
sol: Keepers (soft iron pieces) provide a path for magnetic field lines, preventing 'self-demagnetization'.

q: Which of these is used to make powerful electromagnets?
sol: Soft iron is preferred because it can be magnetized and demagnetized instantly.

q: Which pole of a magnet is usually marked with a red dot or 'N'?
sol: The North Pole is usually distinguished by markings.

q: The substances which are NOT attracted by a magnet are called:
sol: Materials like wood, paper, and glass are non-magnetic.

q: If we bring a piece of wood near a magnet, it will:
sol: Wood is a non-magnetic material.

q: Attraction is a sure test for magnetism.
sol: False. Repulsion is the only sure test, as an unmagnetized iron piece can be attracted by both poles.

q: Unlike poles ________ each other.
sol: Opposite poles (N and S) pull towards each other.

q: A cylindrical magnet has ________ pole(s).
sol: Every magnet, regardless of shape, has exactly two poles.

q: Magnets should be kept away from:
sol: Magnetic fields can interfere with or erase data on electronic devices and storage.

q: The name 'Lodestone' means:
sol: It 'leads' or shows the way (direction), hence the name 'Leading stone'.

q: Which of these is NOT a shape of an artificial magnet?
sol: Artificial magnets are made in specific functional shapes like bar, ring, or horse-shoe.

q: What happens if a magnet is dropped repeatedly?
sol: Rough handling causes the magnetic alignment to break down.

q: Magnetic compass works because of Earth's:
sol: The compass needle aligns with the Earth's natural magnetic field lines.

q: Which material is used to make the needle of a compass?
sol: The needle must be a magnet to react to the Earth's field.

q: Magnets can be made using electricity.
sol: This is how electromagnets are created.

q: A magnet is usually strongest at its:
sol: Magnetic flux is densest at the poles.

q: Paper is a magnetic material.
sol: Paper is non-magnetic.

q: We can see magnetic field lines with our naked eyes.
sol: Magnetic fields are invisible, but we can visualize them using iron filings.

q: Iron filings stick mostly at the ________ of a bar magnet.
sol: Maximum filings accumulate at the poles where attraction is strongest.

q: Which of these is an example of a contact force?
sol: Magnetic force is a non-contact force; friction requires physical contact.

q: Magnets are used in speakers to:
sol: The interaction between a magnet and a coil of wire creates sound vibrations.

q: A horse-shoe magnet has:
sol: It is just a bar magnet bent into a 'U' shape, still having two poles.

q: Materials that get magnetized strongly are called:
sol: Iron, Cobalt, and Nickel are ferromagnetic.

q: The region where a magnet does NOT attract iron filings is:
sol: The center (neutral point) of a bar magnet has almost zero magnetic attraction.

q: Earth's magnetic poles and geographical poles are at the exact same spot.
sol: False. There is a slight tilt/offset between the geographical axis and magnetic axis.

q: Which of these is a temporary magnet?
sol: An electromagnet works only as long as electric current flows through it.

q: If you bring a magnet near a television screen, it might:
sol: Magnets interfere with the electron beams inside older CRT screens.

q: In a bar magnet, the North pole points towards:
sol: By definition, the North-seeking pole points North.

q: A compass can be used to check if a metal rod is a magnet.
sol: If one end of the rod repels a pole of the compass needle, the rod is magnetized.

q: Magnets were discovered in a place called:
sol: The region of Magnesia in Greece is the namesake for 'Magnets'.

q: To make an electromagnet, we need:
o: Battery, iron nail, and copper wire
sol: An electric current flowing through a coil around an iron core creates magnetism.

q: A freely suspended magnet points North-South due to:
sol: It acts like a small needle inside the Earth's massive magnetic field.

q: Magnetic force can pass through:
sol: Magnetic fields can penetrate non-magnetic barriers.

q: Which pole is the 'S' pole of a magnet?
sol: The pole that points towards the Earth's South is the South-seeking pole.

q: Magnetic field is a ________ quantity.
sol: It has both magnitude (strength) and direction.

q: Which of these objects will be attracted by a magnet?
sol: Only the iron nail is magnetic.

q: When we bring a magnet near iron filings, they:
sol: Iron filings are ferromagnetic and stick to the magnet.

q: Does a magnet work in space?
sol: Magnetism is an intrinsic property of the material and does not require an atmosphere.

q: Can two magnetic field lines cross each other?
sol: No, because at the point of intersection, the compass would have to point in two directions, which is impossible.

q: Which of these can be magnetized?
sol: Iron is the most common material for making magnets.

q: Magnets are used in maglev trains to:
sol: Magnetic levitation (Maglev) uses magnetic repulsion to float the train above tracks.

q: What is 'demagnetization'?
sol: It is the process by which a magnet loses its power.

q: What happens when you bring a magnet near a compass?
sol: The magnet's field is stronger than Earth's field nearby, so it pulls the needle.

q: Which pole of a magnet will attract the South pole of another magnet?
sol: Unlike poles attract.

q: An unmagnetized iron bar can be converted into a magnet by:
sol: The 'stroke' method aligns the domains inside the iron.

q: Magnets are used in ________ to separate iron from scrap.
sol: Large electromagnets lift iron and steel from trash.

q: A magnet's attraction is strongest through:
sol: Magnetic force is transmitted most effectively through magnetic materials.

q: Magnetic strength can be increased in an electromagnet by:
o: Increasing number of turns of wire
sol: More current and more coils create a stronger field.

q: Which of these is NOT a magnetic material?
sol: Bronze is an alloy of copper and tin, neither of which is strongly magnetic.

q: Which part of the magnet is the 'neutral' region?
sol: The center of a bar magnet shows little to no attraction.

q: Magnets are used in ATM cards in the form of a:
sol: The black/brown strip on the back stores data magnetically.

q: A magnet that keeps its magnetism for a very long time is:
sol: Permanent magnets are made of hard magnetic materials.

q: If we suspend a magnet vertically, will it point North-South?
sol: Yes, it will align with the Earth's magnetic dip.

q: Artificial magnets were discovered much later than natural magnets.
sol: Lodestones were known for centuries before humans learned to manufacture magnets.

q: Which of these is a property of a magnet?
sol: Magnets attract certain metals and point in specific directions.

q: What happens if a magnet is kept near a heater for long?
sol: High temperature causes demagnetization.

q: The Earth's South Magnetic Pole is near the:
sol: The Earth's magnetic poles are opposite to the geographic names.

q: Which of these is the 'surest test' of magnetism?
sol: Repulsion can only happen between two magnets, while attraction can happen between a magnet and a piece of iron.

q: An electromagnet is a ________ magnet.
sol: It only works when electricity is 'ON'.

q: Magnets in stickers on refrigerators use:
o: Magnetic attraction to the metal door
sol: The magnet attracts the steel/iron body of the fridge.

q: A compass needle is always ________.
sol: It must be a permanent magnet to work.

q: Which pole of a magnet is normally at the top of a compass needle?
sol: The North pole points to the Earth's North.

q: The lines of force around a magnet are called:
sol: These lines represent the direction and strength of the magnetic field.

q: Can we see magnetism?
sol: Magnetism is an invisible force; we only see its effects.

q: Magnetism is a form of:
sol: It is a physical force/phenomenon.

q: Which of these is used to store magnets?
sol: Proper storage prevents magnets from becoming weak.

q: The force between two magnets ________ as the distance increases.
sol: The magnetic force follows an inverse square-like law; distance reduces the pull.

q: Magnets are used in door holders to:
o: Keep the door open/attached to the wall
sol: They hold the door firmly against a metal catch.

q: Lodestone is a ________ magnet.
sol: It is found naturally in the Earth's crust.

q: The word 'Magnet' comes from the Greek region of:
sol: Magnesia was rich in magnetite deposits.
